article_title: Archaeologists Uncover a Previously Unknown Roman Fortlet Beneath Suburban Gardens in Scotland, Expanding the Antonine Wall's Defensive Map

content_preview.title: Archaeologists Uncover a Previously Unknown Roman Fortlet Beneath Suburban Gardens in Scotland, Expanding the Antonine Wall's Defensive Map
content_preview.summary: GUARD Archaeology has published findings from a 2017 excavation in Bearsden that revealed a fortified Roman outpost hidden under residential back gardens, offering the clearest evidence yet of a denser surveillance network along the Antonine Wall than historians had assumed.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A Roman fortlet that garrisoned between 20 and 50 soldiers has been identified beneath suburban back gardens on Boclair Road in Bearsden, roughly five miles northwest of Glasgow. The discovery, [published by GUARD Archaeology](https://www.livescience.com/archaeology/romans/roman-military-fort-discovered-in-scotland-far-north-of-hadrians-wall) in a report titled *ARO65: Discovery of an Antonine Wall Roman Fortlet at Boclair Road, Bearsden*, adds a previously undocumented installation...

editor_notes.overall_assessment: Well-written news article about an archaeological discovery that is unusual for The Machine Herald's typical coverage but demonstrates editorial range. Key claims verified: GUARD Archaeology discovery confirmed by both Live Science and Interesting Engineering, AD 127-247 radiocarbon dating confirmed, suburban garden location in Bearsden confirmed. The article effectively contextualizes the find within the broader archaeology of the Antonine Wall and explains why developer-funded excavations are closing the gap between the historical record and physical evidence.
concerns: Only 2 external sources for an 824-word article is minimal, though both are well-utilized with multiple specific claims attributed to each
The article is categorized as 'News' at 824 words, which is within the upper range for the News category (400-1200)
recommendations: A link to the original GUARD Archaeology report (ARO65) would have strengthened the sourcing
